query: Voyager 1 trajectory correction maneuver TCM thrusters status April 2025

{"query":"Voyager 1 trajectory correction maneuver TCM thrusters status April 2025","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1839,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://spacepolicyonline.com/news/voyager-1-gets-help-from-thrusters-dormant-for-37-years/","title":"Voyager 1 Gets Help from Thrusters Dormant for 37 Years – SpacePolicyOnline.com","content":"NASA’s Voyager 1 spacecraft continues its journey into interstellar space and just got help from on-board thrusters that have not been used for 37 years.
The four Aerojet Rocketdyne MR-103 trajectory correction maneuver (TCM) engines were last fired in 1980 when the spacecraft was passing Saturn.
Voyager 1 was launched in 1977 and flew past Jupiter and Saturn in 1979 and 1980 respectively.
On Tuesday, JPL engineers commanded the thrusters to fire and waited for Voyager’s response.
JPL says Voyager 2’s attitude control thrusters are still fine, but they will likely do a test of its TCM thrusters to determine their status."},{"url":"https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/voyager-1-fires-up-thrusters-after-37-years/","title":"Voyager 1 Fires Up Thrusters After 37 Years | NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL)","content":"But a set of thrusters aboard the Voyager 1 spacecraft successfully fired up Wednesday after 37 years without use.
Now, the Voyager team is able to use a set of four backup thrusters, dormant since 1980.
"With these thrusters that are still functional after 37 years without use, we will be able to extend the life of the Voyager 1 spacecraft by two to three years," said Suzanne Dodd, project manager for Voyager at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, California.
The Voyager team assembled a group of propulsion experts at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, California, to study the problem.
All of Voyager's thrusters were developed by Aerojet Rocketdyne."},{"url":"https://www.adastraspace.com/p/voyager-1-thruster-swap","title":"How NASA swapped Voyager 1's thrusters from 15 billion miles away","content":"How NASA swapped Voyager 1's thrusters from 15 billion miles away
Well, on November 28, 2017, for the first time in 37 years, engineers powered up the TCM thrusters.
Science instruments will have to be shut off well before then, but 2036 is the point Voyager 1 will travel beyond the range of the Deep Space Network and it will no longer be able to communicate with Earth, even if it still has power and the ability to keep itself pointed at the Earth."},{"url":"https://www.popsci.com/voyager-1-thrusters/","title":"Voyager 1 just fired up some thrusters for the first time in 37 years","content":"When Voyager 1’s trajectory correction maneuver thrusters last fired, Ronald Reagan had just been elected president.
After the last course correction for Saturn on November 8, 1980, the TCMs went silent.
The Voyager team expects to have to start flipping off switches in the 2020s, and the probe will likely be completely incommunicado after 2025 or so.
After that, our research probe will turn into more of time capsule."},{"url":"https://www.americaspace.com/2017/12/05/all-fired-up-voyager-1-turns-on-thrusters-for-first-time-in-37-years/","title":"All Fired Up! Voyager 1 Fires Up Thrusters For First Time in 37 Years - AmericaSpace","content":"Dec 5, 2017 — Voyager 1 has remained healthy although its thrusters have been dormant for the past 37 years; the last time they needed to be used was Nov. 8, 1980.Read more
NASA’s Voyager 1 spacecraft is one of the greatest achievements in space exploration, becoming the first probe to reach interstellar space, after epic flybys of Jupiter and Saturn in 1979 and 1980.
The four backup thrusters just fired up hadn’t been used since 1980.
“The Voyager team got more excited each time with each milestone in the thruster test.
Last August, Voyager 2 celebrated 40 years of exploration, long after the flybys of Jupiter and Saturn, as well as Uranus and Neptune, in the late 1970s and 1980s."}]}

Then in 2025, NASA tried to wake them up from 15 billion miles away — knowing that if they fired cold, they could explode, but if they stayed dead, the spacecraft might one day lose its aim at Earth forever.","content":"# For 21 years, Voyager 1’s primary roll thrusters were considered dead. Then in 2025, NASA tried to wake them up from 15 billion miles away — knowing that if they fired cold, they could explode, but if they stayed dead, the spacecraft might one day lose its aim at Earth forever. For Voyager 1, it is a problem stretched across interstellar space, handled by commands that take more than 23 hours to arrive and another 23 hours to report back. That is why NASA’s 2025 recovery of Voyager 1’s long-dormant roll thrusters was more than a clever repair. And the team knew that if the thrusters fired before their heaters came back to life, the result could be a small explosion aboard the most distant operating spacecraft humanity has ever built. For roll, Voyager 1 had been relying on its backup roll thrusters since 2004, when the primary roll thrusters lost power in two small internal heaters and were written off as likely unfixable.","score":0.71377134,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voyager_1","title":"Voyager 1","content":"# *Voyager 1*. | Artist's rendering of the Voyager spacecraft, a small-bodied spacecraft with a large, central dish with multiple arms and antennas extending from the dish Artist's rendering of the *Voyager* spacecraft | |. | Mission type | Outer planetary, heliosphere, and interstellar medium exploration |. ***Voyager 1*** is a space probe launched by NASA on September 5, 1977, as part of the Voyager program, to study the outer Solar System and the interstellar space beyond the Sun's heliosphere. The probe made flybys \"Flyby (spaceflight)\") of Jupiter, Saturn, and Saturn's largest moon, Titan \"Titan (moon)\"). As part of the Voyager program and like its sister craft *Voyager 2*, the spacecraft's extended mission is to locate and study the regions and boundaries of the outer heliosphere and to begin exploring the interstellar medium. In 2017, the Voyager team successfully fired the spacecraft's trajectory correction maneuver (TCM) thrusters for the first time since 1980, enabling the mission to be extended by two to three years.","score":0.6356076,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.popsci.com/voyager-1-thrusters","title":"Voyager 1 just fired up some thrusters for the first time in 37 years","content":"The Voyager team expects to have to start flipping off switches in the 2020s, and the probe will likely be completely incommunicado after 2025","score":0.6083387,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.facebook.com/NASAJPL/posts/the-voyager-1-and-2-spacecraft-are-in-a-planned-communications-pause-as-the-70-m/1226299458863847","title":"Voyager 1 spacecraft thrusters fixed after 2 decades","content":"The Voyager 1 and 2 spacecraft are in a planned communications pause, as the 70-meter antenna that commands them is undergoing upgrades, with only brief","score":0.5920305,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.americaspace.com/2017/12/05/all-fired-up-voyager-1-turns-on-thrusters-for-first-time-in-37-years","title":"All Fired Up!
